contents: REVIEW ARTICLESScrotal Melanoma: A Systematic Review of Presentation,Treatment, and OutcomesJoseph Zikry, BS, Lance W. Chapman, MD, MBA, Dorota Z. Korta, MD, PhD,and Janellen Smith, MD*BACKGROUNDPrimary male genital melanomas are rare. Of these, scrotal melanoma is the least common,and is unfortunately often detected late in the disease course.OBJECTIVETo assess the clinical presentation, treatment modalities, and outcome of scrotal melanoma toaid clinician management.METHODSSystematic literature review of PubMed, yielding 23 cases, of which 20 met the inclusion criteria.RESULTSAlthough previously thought to have the best outcome of genitourinary melanomas, scrotal mela-nomas are associated with high mortality and late presentation. Scrotal melanoma presented as Stage I/II disease18.75% of the time, Stage III 56.3% of the time, and Stage IV 25% of the time, whereas typical cutaneous melanomapresents as Stage I/II disease 84% of the time, Stage III 8% of the time, and Stage IV 4% of the time. Half of patientsexperienced recurrence of their disease. All patients who presented with metastases to distant organs died.LIMITATIONSSmall sample size. Few cases described a Breslow's depth making it difficult to draw con-clusions on tumor thickness and survival outcome.CONCLUSIONScrotal melanoma is rare, aggressive, and is often caught late in the disease course. Theauthors encourage dermatologists to educate patients and destigmatize genital lesions to increase the likeli-hood of earlier detection and better patient outcomes.The authors have indicated no significant interest with commercial supporters.he incidence of malignant melanoma in men iscurrently 2.9%.1 Unlike most malignancies which

are either stable or declining in incidence, the incidenceof melanoma has been steadily increasing.2-4 Primarymale genital melanomas are an extremely rare subset,which comprise penile and scrotal melanomas. Scrotalmelanoma isspecifically theleastprevalent of the genitalmelanomas, with only 23 cases reported in the literatureas of December 2015. Of note, penile melanomas maybe different entities from scrotal melanomas becausethey can involve the mucosal surface of the urethra, andmanagement involves different surgical techniquesbecauseofthesensitiveanatomicallocation.Thisreviewaims to assess the demographics, presenting signs andsymptoms, and treatment modalities of patients withprimary scrotal melanoma as there is currently nosystematic review that does so. Penile melanomas arenot discussed in this review.Materials and MethodsA systematic review on PubMed/MEDLINE wasperformed on December 7, 2015 to gather data onpatients with primary scrotal melanoma. The searchterms used were "scrotal melanoma" and "scrotummelanoma." Inclusion criteria included Englishabstracts and articles pertaining to patients withprimary scrotal melanoma. Only case reports or*All authors are affiliated with the Department of Dermatology, UC Irvine Medical Center, Irvine, California(c) 2017 by the American Society for Dermatologic Surgery, Inc. Published by Wolters Kluwer Health, Inc. All rights reserved.ISSN: 1076-0512* Dermatol Surg 2017;43:765-770* DOI: 10.1097/DSS.0000000000001072case series studies which provided individualpatient information were included. Non-Englisharticles and reply-to-author articles were excluded.Results

Patient PresentationThe mean age of patients with scrotal melanoma was56.6 (range: 31-85). The most common form ofpresentation was a pigmented lesion. Ten percent ofcases presented as cystic scrotal masses. Twenty-fivepercent of the patients presented with an ulceratedlesion. The average size of the lesion on presentationwas 8.2 cm with a range of 1.5 to 15 cm (n = 6). Of the11 cases that specified sidedness, 63.6% were left-sided, 27.3% were right-sided, and 9.1% werecrossing the midline. Forty percent of the patientspresented with inguinal lymphadenopathy. Twenty-five percent of the patients had a known mass for over12 months before seeking medical care. Table 1summarizes patient presentation.Pathological Diagnosis and StagingAmelanotic melanoma was diagnosed in 10% of thecases. Only 11 of the cases described a Breslow's depth,with an average of 7.3 mm (range: 0.55-33 mm). TheAJCC clinical staging could be determined for 80% ofthepatients.InStageI 12.5%presented,6.25%inStageII, 56.3% in Stage III, and 25% in Stage IV. Forty-fivepercent of the patients had melanoma positive inguinallymph nodes on presentation. Twenty percent of thepatients had metastases to distant organs (not includinglymph nodes) at presentation. All patients who pre-sentedwith metastases todistant organs eventually diedof their disease, with an average survival of 8 monthsafter diagnosis (range: 1-19 months).

TreatmentThe mainstay treatment in 95% of patients was widelocal excision (WLE) of their lesion. One patientrefused treatment and died of his disease in 6 months.8Twenty percent of patients had inguinal lymphade-nectomies in addition to local excision. Ten percenthad superficial groin dissections. Ten percent hadorchiectomies in addition to local excision. Twenty-five percent of patients were concomitantly treatedwith chemotherapy in addition to WLE. All thesepatients either had positive lymph nodes or metastasesto distant organs. Of note, Mohs surgery has recentlybeen investigated for the treatment of melanoma insitu and melanoma. However, further research withthis modality is needed before Mohs surgery can beconfidently recommended as the standard of care forscrotal melanoma.A variety and different combination of chemothera-peutic agents were used for patients, including dacar-bazine, cisplatin, visblastine, IL-2, interferon,vincristine, doxorubicin, 5-Fluorouracil, tumor-derived vaccine, vindesine, and temozolomide. Themost common chemotherapeutic agent used wasdacarbazine. Three patients with metastatic diseasewere additionally being treated with radiation.9-11OutcomesSixty percent of patients died of their disease; 5% diedof complications of chemotherapy; 5% died with noTABLE 1. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited.evidence of disease. Twenty-five percent of patientswere alive with no evidence of disease; 5% had nofollow-up. These results are summarized in Table 2. Ofthe 12 patients who died of their disease, the averagelength of time from diagnosis to death was 58.9months (range: 1-461 months). If the individual wholived 461 months9 after diagnosis is considered anoutlier, and not included in the analysis, then theaverage length from diagnosis to death would be 32.1months (range: 1-80 months). Of the 5 patients whohad no evidence of disease when their cases werereported, only 2 of them were followed for more than 5years.9,12 The patient who survived longest afterdiagnosis was alive with no evidence of disease 17years after diagnosis, although he had 2 incidents ofrecurrence at 32 and 60 months.13 Table 3 presentsspecific patient treatment and outcome.Half of patients eventually had recurrence of their dis-ease after initial treatment. Sites of metastasis andrecurrence include the pelvis, brain, skeletal system,liver, lungs, abdominal wall, and skin. Ninety percentof recurrences occurred within 3 years (average = 17.5months), and one case seemed to be an outlier withrecurrence occurring at 432 months after diagnosis.The average time from recurrence to death was22 months (range: 0-56 months). Eighty percentof patients who had recurrence were treated with che-motherapy. Only 1 patient had genetic tumor analysiscompleted and was reported to be KIT negative.16

DiscussionScrotal melanoma is recognized as the rarest form ofgenitourinary melanoma. Due to the paucity of dataon scrotal melanoma in the literature, there are nocurrent guidelines to aid clinicians in its diagnosis ormanagement.The most common form of presentation for a scrotalmelanoma was a raised pigmented lesion; however, itcan also present as an ulcerated lesion, a cystic mass,or a mass with satellite lesions. Three of 20 cases wereinitially misdiagnosed as either an epidermal inclu-sion cyst, benign scrotal cyst, or a blood blister.12,19Therefore, a thorough and broad differential diag-nosis should always be constructed for scrotallesions, with a consideration for both benign andmalignant etiologies. Examples include melanocyticnevi, bowenoid papulosis, Bowen's disease, extra-mammary Paget's disease, squamous cell carcino-mas, and pigmented basal cell carcinomas.17 Foramelanotic and cystic lesions, the differential diag-nosis becomes even more comprehensive to includemany diseases of mesenchymal origin. Lip-osarcomas, leimyosarcomas, rhabdomyosarcomas,malignant fibrous histiocytomas, epidermal inclu-sion cysts, mesotheliomas, and lymphomas areamong those that should be included.20

Once the diagnosis of scrotal melanoma has beenmade, treatment options should be offered to patients.First, a thorough oculocutaneous examination shouldbe performed to ensure that the scrotal melanoma is infact the primary malignancy. Next, a Breslow's depthmust be established to aid in determining excisionmargins and staging of the melanoma. Unfortunately,one limitation in this study is that only 55% of thecases in this review reported a Breslow's depth, mak-ing it difficult to make concrete conclusions on tumorthickness and clinical outcome.The gold standard treatment of scrotal melanomaand the therapy offered to 100% of patients in thisreview is WLE of the lesion. No patient in this reviewhad local recurrence of their disease. For local exci-sion, the authors believe that the National Compre-hensive Cancer Network (NCCN) offers sufficientguidelines to determine margins based on tumorthickness.21 Furthermore, it is important to assessneighboring structures within the scrotum and clini-cal judgment should be used to determine whetherany additional structures should be removed.For example, 2 of the patients in this review hadconcomitant orchiectomies.TABLE 2. Scrotal Melanoma Survival OutcomeSurvival Outcome (n = 20)Result, %Died of disease60Alive, no evidence of disease25Died of chemotherapy complications5Died, no evidence of disease5

No follow-up5(c) 2017 by the American Society for Dermatologic Surgery, Inc. Published by Wolters Kluwer Health, Inc. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited.Next, the physician must assess lymph nodes.According to the NCCN Practice Guidelines forOncology, physicians should consider a sentinellymph node biopsy for Stage IA melanoma withadverse features (e.g. lymphovascular invasion,tumor-infiltrating lymphocytes) and encourage senti-nel lymph node biopsy for Stage IB or II.21 Patient'swith Stage III disease should be offered lymph nodedissection according to guidelines. Although allpatients in this review who underwent local excisionachieved effective control from local recurrence, thesame cannot be said about regional and distantrecurrence. Of the 10 patients who presented with noclinical adenopathy and no distant metastases who didnot receive inguinal node staging, 70% had eitherregional recurrence or distant metastases. The major-ity, 71% (5/7) of those cases included regionalrecurrence.

It was previously thought that scrotal melanomasmay be the least aggressive and have the best out-come out of all genitourinary melanomas.12 How-ever, this was based on a case series with only 2patients who were alive and with no evidence ofdisease after excision. Although the limited samplesize in this review makes it difficult to draw strongconclusions on the aggressiveness of scrotal mela-nomas, their tendency to present late in the diseasecourse is more conclusive. Typical cutaneous mela-noma patients present with localized cutaneous dis-ease (Stage I or II) in 84% of cases,22 whereas just18.75% (n = 16) of the patients with scrotal mela-noma presented with Stage I or II disease. Likewise,only 8% of typical melanoma patients present withStage III disease, compared with an alarming 56.3%of scrotal patients. Last, just 4% of typical cutaneousmelanoma patients presented with Stage IV diseaseTABLE 3. Published by Wolters Kluwer Health, Inc. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited.(distant metastases), whereas 25% of scrotal mela-noma patients presented with Stage 4 disease, furtherproviding evidence of their late presentation. Table 4presents information on the patients who presentedwith metastatic disease to distant organs. Becausevery few patients in this review were shown to sur-vive greater than 5 years it is difficult to compare andassess survival outcomes of scrotal melanoma totypical cutaneous melanoma. Because more cases arereported in the literature, it may become more clear ifthe poor outcome of scrotal melanomas can beattributed to a more aggressive nature or solely toa later presentation.

Another issue worthy of discussion is both thepatient's and clinician's comfort in dealing withgenital lesions. Twenty-five percent of the patients inthis review had a known mass for over 12 monthsbefore seeking medical care. Equally disturbing, 3patients in this review had a mass that was 10 cm orgreater. A study on male genital lesions showed that65% of patients had a delay of 6 months beforeconsulting a medical professional, citing embarrass-ment because of symptoms in a sexualized area as theprimary reason.23 In addition, 50% of the patients inthat study did not even discuss the problem with theirpartners. It has also been shown that men have lessknowledge of cancer symptoms and more passivedetection behavior than women.24 Therefore, theauthors believe that it is vital for both dermatologists,urologists, and primary care physicians to routinelyeducate and discuss genital malignancies with malepatients in effort to shorten the delay of diagnosisand to destigmatize the subject. A study on full-bodyskin examinations (FBSEs) highlights this point byshowing that patient education on the objectives andbenefits of a FBSE decreases patient concern aboutreceiving a genital examination.25 Furthermore, itwas shown that only 42% of patients reportedexamining themselves for skin cancer.26 The authorshope that with education and destigmatization ofgenital lesions, patients will be encouraged to seekearly care for concerning scrotal lesions, thusincreasing the likelihood of having improvedoutcomes because of less advanced disease onpresentation.

As scrotal melanoma is an extremely rare entity, thisposed challenges and limitations to this study. First, thisstudy is limited with respect to a small sample size.There were only 23 reported cases, of which 20 casesmet criteria for inclusion. Another limitation is that allthe data were derived from case reports, putting theliterature at risk for potential bias. However, as thisdisease is so rare, there is currently no other means togather information on scrotal melanoma. Therefore,although these limitations do in fact exist, the authorsstill consider it worthwhile to gather and analyze thedataof thesecasereportsinaggregateasthisiscurrentlythe only way to learn more about this rare disease.ConclusionIn conclusion, although scrotal melanomas are rare,their late presentation and subsequent poor outcomemakes it imperative that dermatologists educate theirpatients and destigmatize genital lesions to increasethe likelihood of earlier detection and better patientoutcomes.References1.
